readings: Modern Japan Combined: Consumer Revolution, Income-Doubling Plan, Minamata, Middle Class Decline, Japanese Feminism

readingAnalyses.title: The Consumer Revolution in Postwar Japan (1960)
readingAnalyses.author: Economic Planning Agency
readingAnalyses.source: White Paper on the People's Livelihood, 1960
readingAnalyses.summary: The 1960 White Paper captures official optimism about Japan's 'lifestyle revolution' - the rapid transformation of consumption patterns through Westernization of food, clothing, and housing. The document identifies five factors driving change: rising incomes, technological revolution (TV, washing machines, refrigerators), new mass consumers from compressed income inequality, value shifts from frugality to enjoyment, and aggressive marketing. Big-company salaryman households emerge as the emblem of this new middle class.

keyQuotes.quote: Looking at the transformed consumption patterns among the people of the past few years, one sees numerous radical changes. No one at the recovery stage of the postwar years, not to mention the prewar era, could have imagined this.

From the Tokugawa era to World War II, our people's basic attitude toward life focused on diligent saving and restrained consumption. Frugality was among the great virtues of everyday life. After the war... values of frugality gave way to the idea of enjoying life.
Some even argue that all demand for goods beyond the basic necessities of clothing, food, and housing is in a sense created by the producers through advertising.

thematicExamples: The 'Three Sacred Treasures' of postwar consumerism: TV sets, washing machines, and refrigerators - owned at rate of one per two households in large cities by 1960
Shift from extended to nuclear families enabling new patterns of consumption and two-income couples
Government concern about consumer protection as new goods 'appear one after another' - already worried about advertising manipulation
realLifeConnections: Parallels to contemporary debates about consumerism, advertising's power, and whether 'demand is created by producers'
Japan's postwar value shift from frugality to consumption mirrors debates in other developing economies today
Questions about whether rising incomes and consumption actually equal wellbeing remain relevant

readingAnalyses.title: The Income-Doubling Plan (1961-1970)
readingAnalyses.author: Economic Planning Agency
readingAnalyses.source: New Long-Range Economic Plan of Japan
readingAnalyses.summary: Prime Minister Ikeda Hayato's ambitious 1961 plan aimed to double GNP within ten years while reducing inequality between urban/rural, large/small enterprises, and high/low income groups. The plan exemplifies Japan's state-guided capitalism: government set growth targets, specified priority industries (heavy and chemical, especially machinery), recommended mergers, called for interfirm cooperation, and committed to 'supplement and guide private enterprises.' This developmental state model became influential worldwide.

keyQuotes.quote: The Doubling National Income Plan should have an objective of achieving full employment and radically raising people's living standard by rapidly doubling the gross national product.

The machinery industry is most counted upon not only as an export industry but also for its ability to absorb surplus labor. In other words, the industry will occupy an important 'strategic' position for a rapid growth of economy.
The government must take adequate measures to supplement and guide private enterprises.

thematicExamples: Specific investment targets: ¥16 trillion total over 10 years, with ¥5.5 trillion for machinery, ¥3 trillion for iron/steel, ¥2.5 trillion for chemicals
Government role: modernize equipment, establish mass production, standardize products, promote mergers, train scientists and technicians
The shift from constitutional revision and suppressing the left to promoting economic development - growth as political strategy
realLifeConnections: The 'developmental state' model influenced Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, and continues to shape debates about industrial policy
Contemporary debates about government's role in guiding economic development (e.g., US CHIPS Act, green industrial policy)
Questions about whether state-guided capitalism produces faster growth than free market approaches

readingAnalyses.title: Environmental Activism: Minamata Disease
readingAnalyses.author: Sit-in Strike Declaration (Waga Shimin)
readingAnalyses.summary: The Minamata readings document Japan's most devastating environmental disaster: mercury poisoning from Chisso Corporation's chemical plant in Kyushu. Beginning in the 1950s, methylmercury discharged into Minamata Bay bioaccumulated in fish, causing horrific neurological damage to fishing communities. For years, Chisso denied responsibility while the government prioritized economic growth. The title 'Waga Shimin' is a grim pun - shimin normally means 'citizens' but here is written with characters meaning 'dead people.' The document captures victims' sense of betrayal by both corporation and state.

keyQuotes.quote: As we who suffer from Minamata disease and our families continue our sit-in strike in front of the company president's office at Chisso headquarters [in Tokyo], how can we possibly thank the many people throughout the nation who have provided us with their warm support?
Although we began our negotiations with Chisso this past December 6, we have met nothing but a scheme to avoid us, in the form of a note mentioning the company president's (feigned?) sickness.

thematicExamples: The title's bitter wordplay: 'We Citizens' becomes 'We Dead People' - victims already considered dead by the system that poisoned them
Corporate denial tactics: feigned presidential illness to avoid meeting victims who traveled from Kyushu to Tokyo
Grassroots organizing from the late 1960s succeeded where earlier efforts failed, as economic growth made compensation easier to afford
realLifeConnections: Minamata parallels contemporary environmental justice movements from Flint to Cancer Alley - who bears the costs of industrial development?
The 2011 Fukushima nuclear disaster raised similar questions about corporate responsibility, government oversight, and prioritizing growth over safety
Global debates about climate change as distributional justice - the poor and marginalized bear disproportionate costs

readingAnalyses.title: The Japanese Middle Class at Century's End
readingAnalyses.author: Yada Giichi
readingAnalyses.summary: After a decade of economic stagnation following the 1990 bubble collapse, social commentary turned gloomy. Yada's newspaper feature captures the new pessimism: the stability of middle-class 'mainstream consciousness' (churyu ishiki) was crumbling. Jobs of male breadwinners seemed insecure; regular full-time employment was giving way to temp and contract work; real estate and stock values were plunging. The article documents the erosion of Japan's postwar social contract - lifetime employment, steady advancement, home ownership - that had defined middle-class success.

keyQuotes.quote: For all of us who have been firmly confident about our mainstream life, the ground beneath our feet is shaking.

The stability of the work environment is crumbling. Real-estate prices, counted on as a source of collateral and capital, are plunging... It is impossible for a regular full-time employee to continue at a job with any sense of security. There are no grounds for optimism.
In a survey conducted for this report, 64 percent of businesses replied that the category of employees engaged in 'activities requiring long-term accumulated skills' had shrunk.

thematicExamples: The shift from 80% to 70% lifetime employees predicted - meaning more temp, contract, and precarious work
Temp work expanding from 'women's work' to include men at construction sites and warehouses
The 'Lost Decades' challenging triumphalist narratives of Japanese exceptionalism
realLifeConnections: The 'gig economy' and precarious employment are now global phenomena, not uniquely Japanese
Japan's demographic crisis (aging population, low birthrate) partly attributed to economic insecurity making family formation difficult
Questions about whether the postwar 'social contract' of stable employment was historically anomalous

readingAnalyses.title: Japanese Feminism: Hiratsuka Raicho and the 'New Woman'
readingAnalyses.author: Hiratsuka Raicho
readingAnalyses.summary: Hiratsuka Raicho founded Seito (Bluestocking), Japan's first feminist journal, in 1911. Her famous declaration 'In the beginning, woman was the sun' proclaimed women's original power and autonomy. By 1913, her definition of the 'New Woman' included explicit critique of male privilege: women were 'made ignorant, made a slave, made a piece of meat by male selfishness.' The new woman seeks to destroy old morality and laws created for male advantage and create a new kingdom based on women's spiritual values.

keyQuotes.quote: The new woman; I am a new woman. I seek, I strive each day to be that truly new woman I want to be. In truth, that eternally new being is the sun. I am the sun.
The new woman is not satisfied with the life of the kind of woman who is made ignorant, made a slave, made a piece of meat by male selfishness.
The new woman seeks to destroy the old morality and laws created for male advantage... day by day attempts to create a new kingdom, where a new religion, a new morality, and new laws are carried out.
The new woman today seeks neither beauty nor virtue. She is simply crying out for strength, the strength to fulfill her own hallowed mission.

thematicExamples: The sun metaphor: reclaiming Amaterasu (sun goddess) as symbol of women's original power before patriarchal suppression
Bluestocking as Japan's first journal 'created for the first time with the brains and hands of today's Japanese women'
The demand for 'strength' rather than 'beauty' or 'virtue' - rejecting traditional feminine ideals
realLifeConnections: Hiratsuka's rhetoric anticipates later waves of feminism worldwide - the personal is political, destroy the master's house
The tension between 'individualist' and 'maternalist' feminism continues in contemporary debates about work-family balance
Her 1913 manifesto resonates with #MeToo's critique of male entitlement to women's bodies

readingAnalyses.title: Ueno Chizuko: Feminism, Orientalism, and National Identity
readingAnalyses.author: Ueno Chizuko
readingAnalyses.source: Are the Japanese Feminine? Some Problems of Japanese Feminism in Its Cultural Context
readingAnalyses.summary: Sociologist Ueno Chizuko examines how Japanese feminism is trapped between Orientalism and 'reverse Orientalism.' When Japan is characterized as 'feminine' relative to the 'masculine' West, feminists face a dilemma: demand equal rights and be called anti-Japanese, or stress feminine virtues and have men reply 'we're already feminine enough.' She traces this through the case of Takamure Itsue, a major prewar feminist who embraced 'maternalist feminism' as distinctly Japanese - but whose anti-Western countermodernism eventually led her to support fascism and war.

keyQuotes.quote: In the concept of Orientalism proposed by Edward Said, the East is to the West as woman is to man. Taking an Orientalist approach to national identity, Japanese intellectuals have described Japan as an oppressed woman.

If feminists ask for equal rights, they are accused of being anti-Japanese; on the other hand, if they stress feminine virtues, men think it over and say, 'Look, in contrast to Western men, we are already feminine enough. Why do we have to become more feminine?'
Takamure wrote: 'The maternal self sees humankind as one family. As our sacred war is a challenge to what interferes with this familyhood, the war is ours, for women.'
We find ourselves trapped in a vicious circle that oscillates between Orientalism and a reverse Orientalism. Feminism as a cultural product cannot escape from this closed framework.

thematicExamples: Takamure's trajectory: from feminist historian seeking matriarchy in ancient Japan to wartime collaborator embracing fascism as 'liberating for women'
The Heian period as origin of 'Japanese' (vs. Chinese) culture - and how women's kana writing created the vernacular tradition
The emperor as 'mother of the nation' and 'empty center' - the feminization of Japanese national identity
realLifeConnections: The accusation that feminism is 'Western import' appears across Asia and the Global South - how do feminists respond?
Debates about 'difference feminism' vs. 'equality feminism' continue globally
The risk that cultural particularism leads to nationalism - relevant wherever identity politics meets national identity

readingAnalyses.title: Saito Chiyo: What is Japanese Feminism?
readingAnalyses.author: Saito Chiyo
readingAnalyses.summary: Saito Chiyo, founder of the feminist journal Agora, offers her own definition of feminism for the late twentieth century: 'An international cultural and human rights movement to cleanse experience and knowledge, a movement that adopts a female perspective rather than the traditional male value system. Opposition to the privileging of production and efficiency models. The privileging of human life and sexuality. The elimination of all discrimination.' She emphasizes this definition may change and invites disagreement.

keyQuotes.quote: An international cultural and human rights movement to 'cleanse' experience and knowledge, a movement that adopts a female perspective rather than the traditional male value system.

Opposition to the privileging of production and efficiency models. The privileging of human life and sexuality. The elimination of all discrimination based on gender, economic status, race, culture, education, etc.
This would be my definition for the present, but it might well change in the future. It is a definition that is informed by all my own priorities, and so I would invite others to disagree.

thematicExamples: The explicit rejection of 'production and efficiency models' as male values - connects to environmental and anti-capitalist critiques
Feminism as 'international' movement - pushing back against accusations of Western import
The humility of 'this might change' and 'I invite others to disagree' - feminism as process not dogma
realLifeConnections: Contemporary intersectional feminism similarly emphasizes eliminating 'all discrimination' across categories
Debates about whether feminism is inherently anti-capitalist or can work within market systems
The question of how to define feminism remains contested and evolving

overallSynthesis:
These readings together reveal that Japan's postwar 'economic miracle' was not simply a triumph of development but a contested achievement with profound costs distributed unequally across society. The optimistic 1960 White Paper and Income-Doubling Plan capture official ideology at its peak: Japan would double its GNP, create mass consumers, and guide industry toward international competitiveness. The state-guided developmental model worked spectacularly - for a time.

But the costs were real. The Minamata case shows how rapid industrialization poisoned communities while corporations and government officials prioritized growth over human welfare. The fishermen and their families who suffered were dismissed as obstacles to progress, their bodies bearing the toxic burden of Japan's ascent. The bitter wordplay of the document's title - 'We Citizens' becoming 'We Dead People' - captures victims' understanding that they had been sacrificed.

The feminist readings reveal parallel critiques. Hiratsuka Raicho's 1911-1913 manifestos declared war on a system that made women 'ignorant, slaves, pieces of meat.' Ueno Chizuko's sophisticated analysis shows how feminism gets trapped between Orientalism and reverse Orientalism - if Japan is characterized as 'feminine,' then feminists demanding equality are 'anti-Japanese' while those stressing feminine virtues reinforce the dominant paradigm. The cautionary tale of Takamure Itsue - a genuine feminist whose maternalism and cultural particularism led her to embrace fascism - demonstrates how easily progressive movements can be captured by nationalism.

By the 1990s, the miracle had ended. Yada Giichi's 'Farewell, Mainstream Consciousness!' captures the collapse of the postwar social contract. The stability of lifetime employment, rising incomes, and home ownership that defined middle-class success was giving way to precarious temp work and 'lost decades' of stagnation. Japan's model, once celebrated as superior to Western capitalism, seemed to have reached its limits.

As the final content plate, this session invites students to reconsider everything they've learned: national 'success' always has winners and losers, development models embed particular values and power structures, and the people who bear the costs - whether pollution victims, women, or precarious workers - must organize and fight to have their stories heard.
executiveSummary:
Japan's postwar economic miracle transformed a devastated nation into the world's second-largest economy. The 1960 White Paper and Income-Doubling Plan capture official optimism: state-guided capitalism would double GNP, create mass consumers, and make Japan internationally competitive. But growth came with costs: environmental destruction (Minamata), persistent gender inequality, and eventually economic stagnation (the 'Lost Decades').

The readings present multiple critical perspectives. Minamata victims' 'Sit-in Strike Declaration' exposes corporate denial and government complicity - growth prioritized over human lives. The feminist readings trace struggles from Hiratsuka Raicho's 1911 'I am the sun' declaration through Ueno Chizuko's analysis of how feminism gets trapped between Orientalism and nationalism. Takamure Itsue's trajectory - from feminist historian to wartime collaborator - warns how easily progressive movements can be captured by cultural particularism.

This final plate asks students to consider the full picture: What did Japan's 'success' actually mean? For whom? At what cost?

keywords.definition: 1961 government plan to double GNP in 10 years through state-guided industrial policy
keywords.relevance: Exemplifies developmental state model; Japan actually exceeded the target

keywords.definition: Mercury poisoning from Chisso Corporation's industrial waste; thousands of victims
keywords.relevance: Symbol of environmental costs of rapid industrialization; landmark citizen activism

keywords.definition: White-collar male employee devoted to company; icon of postwar middle class
keywords.relevance: Represents both economic success and human costs (overwork, conformity, gendered division of labor)

keywords.definition: 'Mainstream consciousness' - identification as middle class shared by 90% of Japanese
keywords.relevance: Its erosion in the 1990s signaled end of postwar social contract

keywords.definition: Period of economic stagnation from 1990 bubble burst to present
keywords.relevance: Challenged triumphalist narratives; Japan's model had limits

keywords.definition: Japan's first feminist journal, founded by Hiratsuka Raicho in 1911
keywords.relevance: Launched modern Japanese feminism; 'In the beginning, woman was the sun'

keywords.definition: Revaluing 'Oriental' traits positively but within same binary framework
keywords.relevance: Ueno's analysis of how cultural particularism can become nationalism

keywords.definition: Feminism emphasizing motherhood and feminine virtues vs. individual rights
keywords.relevance: Takamure Itsue's version led to supporting fascism as 'liberating for women'

keyPassages.quote: From the Tokugawa era to World War II, our people's basic attitude toward life focused on diligent saving and restrained consumption. Frugality was among the great virtues of everyday life. After the war... values of frugality gave way to the idea of enjoying life.
keyPassages.source: White Paper on the People's Livelihood, 1960

keyPassages.quote: For all of us who have been firmly confident about our mainstream life, the ground beneath our feet is shaking... It is impossible for a regular full-time employee to continue at a job with any sense of security. There are no grounds for optimism.
The new woman is not satisfied with the life of the kind of woman who is made ignorant, made a slave, made a piece of meat by male selfishness.

If feminists ask for equal rights, they are accused of being anti-Japanese; on the other hand, if they stress feminine virtues, men think it over and say, 'Look, in contrast to Western men, we are already feminine enough.'
The maternal self sees humankind as one family. As our sacred war is a challenge to what interferes with this familyhood, the war is ours, for women.

people: Ikeda Hayato - PM who shifted LDP strategy from constitutional revision to economic growth; Income-Doubling Plan
Hiratsuka Raicho - Founded Seito (Bluestocking) 1911; 'I am the sun' declaration; maternalist feminist
Ueno Chizuko - Sociologist; first female professor at Tokyo University; analyzed feminism and Orientalism
places: Minamata - Kyushu fishing town; site of mercury poisoning disaster; symbol of environmental costs
Tokyo - Economic center; Chisso headquarters where victims staged sit-in strike
Heian-period Japan - Origin of 'Japanese' (vs. Chinese) culture; women developed kana writing
things: Three Sacred Treasures - TV, washing machine, refrigerator; symbols of consumer revolution
Developmental state model - State-guided capitalism; government sets targets, guides industry
Reverse Orientalism - Revaluing 'Eastern' traits positively but within same binary framework

keyQuestions.question: The 1960 White Paper celebrates 'values of frugality gave way to the idea of enjoying life.' Is this progress? What gets lost when traditional values change?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Connect to debates about consumerism, sustainability, meaning. Does consumption equal wellbeing? What about the claim that demand is 'created by producers through advertising'? Students may have personal experience with family generational differences in attitudes toward consumption.

keyQuestions.question: Takamure Itsue was a genuine feminist who ended up supporting fascism by arguing 'the war is ours, for women.' How did this happen? Could it happen again?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Trace the logic: maternalist feminism → cultural particularism → anti-Westernism → nationalism → fascism. The lesson is how easily progressive movements can be captured when they define themselves against 'the West' rather than for specific principles. Relevant to contemporary debates about identity politics and nationalism.

keyQuestions.question: As we conclude the course, what have we learned about 'modernity' in East Asia? Return to Plate 3's questions about what modernity means.
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Modernity meant different things in different places. It brought both liberation (consumer choice, women's movements) and new forms of oppression (Minamata, gender inequality). There is no single path. The 'economic miracle' had costs. 'Success' depends on who's counting and what counts. The story isn't over - Japan's 'lost decades' continue, feminism continues, environmental movements continue.

timeline.event: Hiratsuka Raicho founds Seito (Bluestocking); 'I am the sun' declaration
Minamata disease first recognized; Chisso denies responsibility
White Paper celebrates 'consumer revolution'; TV/washing machine ownership soars
Income-Doubling Plan launched; aim to double GNP by 1970
Kumamoto court holds Chisso liable; landmark environmental ruling
Bubble peaks and bursts; beginning of 'Lost Decades'

connections.fromPrevious: Plate 24 examined Korean development under division. Japan's postwar model influenced Korea (state-guided, export-oriented) but with different outcomes - Japan's earlier success led to complacency, while Korea continued to catch up.
connections.toNext: This is the final content plate. Next session: student presentations! Prepare to synthesize what you've learned across the course.
connections.broaderThemes: The course has traced East Asia from traditional foundations through Western encounter, nationalism, war, and reconstruction. We end by asking: What have these societies gained and lost? The 'economic miracle' narrative obscures as much as it reveals. Environmental victims, women, precarious workers - their stories complicate triumphalist accounts.

nextPreview.preview: Students present their research projects. Synthesize what you've learned; connect past to present; make an argument about significance.

teachingNotes: SEVEN distinct primary/secondary sources in this reading - budget time for each. The consumer revolution and Income-Doubling Plan are fastest; feminism is most complex.
The 'Waga Shimin' wordplay (citizens/dead people) is powerful - explain it! The victims understood they'd been sacrificed for growth.
Hiratsuka's 'I am the sun' is the most quotable passage. 'Made ignorant, made a slave, made a piece of meat' still resonates.
Ueno Chizuko's analysis is sophisticated - the dilemma of feminism when Japan is already characterized as 'feminine' is genuinely difficult. Don't oversimplify.

Takamure's trajectory is the cautionary tale: cultural particularism → nationalism → fascism. 'As our sacred war is a challenge to what interferes with familyhood, the war is ours, for women.' How did a feminist end up here?
The 1997 'Farewell, Mainstream Consciousness' predicts much of what happened since - precarious employment, eroding middle class, lost decades. Japan as preview of Western futures?
Return to Plate 3's questions about modernity. Full circle: what is modernity? Whose modernity? At what cost?
